title: 1.1: Reports the serialized IPv4 packet length as 20 bytes, demonstrating it treated the packet as a minimal IPv4 header without options.
1.2: Reports first 20 bytes of serialized IPv4 header in hexadecimal format (e.g., 45 00 00 14...), demonstrating reliance on actual on-the-wire bytes.
1.3: Explains that the last four bytes of the header hex sequence decode to the chosen destination IPv4 address, demonstrating understanding of how Scapy encodes that field.
1.4: States that accessing pkt.dst at runtime returns a Python string in dotted-decimal form, demonstrating awareness of the high-level interface for IPv4 destinations.

1.5: Reports that clearly invalid destination inputs raise an address resolution error (e.g., socket.gaierror or 'Name not known') during packet construction, demonstrating understanding of when validation happens in practice.
1.8: Identifies IPField as the field class used to represent IPv4 address values, demonstrating awareness of where address semantics are centralized.
1.9: States that IPField.i2m converts Python-level IPv4 values into the 4-byte on-the-wire representation, demonstrating understanding of the field’s serialization hook.
1.10: Explains that IPField.i2m emits an all-zero IPv4 address when the value is None, demonstrating awareness of how missing values are encoded.
1.11: Explains that IPField.h2i validates string inputs using a low-level IPv4 parser(such as inet_aton()), demonstrating that plain dotted addresses go through an explicit validation step.
1.12: States that the IPv4 destination field uses a DestIPField built on top of IPField, demonstrating recognition of specialization for destination handling.
1.13: Explains that DestIPField can derive a destination value from packet context when no explicit destination is provided, demonstrating awareness of its defaulting behavior.

1.14: Explains that fields like IPField register themselves with packet classes via a registration hook(such as register_owner()) called when new packet types are defined, demonstrating understanding of how fields join the type system.
1.15: Explains that resolution of Net-style destinations can fail during system lookups and surface as address-resolution errors, demonstrating linkage between Net handling and runtime failures.
1.16: Reports that the TTL value was explicitly set in the packet construction (e.g., ttl=64), showing awareness of how to configure IPv4 header fields.
1.17: Reports that at least one IPv4 flag was set in the packet (e.g., flags="DF"), showing the ability to manipulate flag bits in the header.
1.18: Explains that DestIPField uses IPField’s conversion logic to turn its destination value into bytes, demonstrating that specialization reuses the base encoding method.
1.19: Mentions the fields_desc attribute where packet classes declare their field instances, demonstrating understanding of how fields are associated with packets.
